week.label: Week of Mon 22 Jun 2026 · generated 24 Jun 2026 · updated weekly from IFR / LSEG LPC
week.lead: Malaysia's data-centre boom is testing how much its lenders can actually hold. DayOne is back for ~US$3.7bn — bigger than the US$3.46bn it closed a year ago — while AirTrunk (US$2.325bn) and Princeton Digital pile on, taking 2026's Malaysian DC borrowings well past 2025's US$8bn. The catch: the same seven hyperscaler offtakers keep recurring, loan-to-cost is creeping above 90%, and banks are hitting single-name, sector and country exposure limits. The question on every desk — who is left to fund the next campus, and at a spread that pays for the risk?
week.wnLabel: · week of Mon 22 Jun 2026 — vs the Jun 15 reading
week.wnNote: The lead rotates from Taiwan's chip-borrowing surge (Jun 15) to Malaysia's data-centre jumbo loans — and the credit & regulatory limits now straining lenders. Same AI thread, but the pressure point moves from the chip up to the data centre that runs it.

whatsnew.t: Lead flips from Taiwan semis (Jun 15) to Malaysia's data-centre boom — and the lender-capacity strain behind it.
AirTrunk launches a US$2.325bn PF for 198MW of its JHB2 Johor campus (~225bp/SOFR, ~12 banks) — a fresh jumbo alongside DayOne's ~US$3.7bn.
Princeton Digital closes US$456m for the JC3 Jakarta campus — 12 lenders, green loan, +US$400m accordion.
CTBC steps up as a lead: arranging SPIL's NT$40bn half of ASE's NT$80bn, and an MLAB on Apollo's ¥360bn NSG buyout (listed as 'CBC Bank').
Australia cluster lands: Endeavour Energy A$1.8bn, Perth Airport A$500m, Transurban A$825m, Tabcorp A$980m — plus EQT's A$145m Fitness Passport add-on (CTBC a relationship lender).
Gunvor's Singapore SLL upsized to US$1.37bn from a US$800m launch — 32 banks at just 70bp over SOFR.
Banks warn Asia hasn't repriced: DC spreads ticked up in the US/Europe but 'in Asia it hasn't', even as exposure limits bite globally.

DayOne's new Johor borrowing — Asia's biggest DC draw, after US$3.46bn last year
Johor DC capacity planned at end-2025 — largest in Asia Pacific (Cushman & Wakefield)
Loan-to-cost on the largest hyperscaler PFs (Oracle/Vantage, US) — leverage creep
Range of all-in spreads in the reading, Gunvor SLL → Fitness Passport LBO

week.ctbcCap: CTBC's footprint across this week's deals

ctbcReport.t: CTBC Bank is lead arranger for SPIL's NT$40bn half of ASE's NT$80bn (US$2.52bn) jumbo — Bank of Taiwan leads the ASE half.
CTBC (listed as 'CBC Bank') is an MLAB on the ¥360bn (US$2.25bn) LBO loan — a seat on Apollo's largest-ever Japan buyout.
CTBC was an MLAB on the A$500m 2025 LBO loan; EQT now seeks a A$145m incremental — CTBC a relationship lender.
Context: Asia's most active retail lenders treat DC loans as real-estate exposure; Article 72-2 caps that at 30% of deposits, so CTBC & peers stay small-ticket.

week.ladderCap: Approximate all-in margins over benchmark (bp) seen this week — a map of where risk sits

ladder.who: Top commodity trader — Gunvor US$1.37bn SLL (over SOFR; 140bp all-in)
IG infrastructure — Endeavour Energy, Perth Airport (over BBSY)
Malaysia DC project loans — AirTrunk, DayOne (over SOFR / Klibor)
Sponsor LBO — Apollo / Nippon Sheet Glass (over Tonar)
Sub-IG LBO — EQT / Fitness Passport (over BBSY; ~5.3×)

risks.p: Jumbo single-site DC asks of US$3bn–4bn collide with banks' single-name, sector and country caps. The same ~7 hyperscaler offtakers recur, so offtaker limits bind too — some banks are already 'full' on Oracle.
DC leases run 7–10yr (5+5) but renewal is at the offtaker's discretion. No lease-renewal cycle has yet been observed; if technology shifts, hyperscalers can walk away.
Loan-to-cost is rising above 90% (US Vantage PFs ~93–98%), covenants watered down to little more than a debt cap, with guarantees and change-of-control removed (DayOne dropped its parent guarantee).
As banks keep underwriting, spreads ticked up in the US and Europe but 'in Asia it hasn't' — Asian DC margins lag even as exposure limits tighten globally.
Asia's most active retail lenders treat DC loans as real-estate exposure; Article 72-2 of the Banking Act caps that at 30% of deposits, so they stay small-ticket — thinning the natural buyer base.
Deals with ByteDance / PRC links draw a limited lender pool on geopolitics; they lean on relationship PRC & HK banks (six PRC banks took US$450m of DayOne's US$790m sell-down last year).
